era_context: The Roman Empire under Caligula (37–41 CE), whose reign was marked by increasing erraticism and megalomania. Judea under Roman procuratorial rule, with Herod Agrippa I rising in prominence. The early Christian movement in its earliest phase, centered in Jerusalem and Judea. Han Dynasty China under Guangwu, consolidating power after the collapse of the Western Han.

events.title: Caligula's Reign Becomes Increasingly Erratic
events.description: By 38 CE, Caligula's behavior had become increasingly unstable and autocratic. He began to demand divine honors, declared himself a god, and initiated a series of purges against the Senate and wealthy aristocrats. His spending on lavish projects and games depleted the imperial treasury. These actions alienated the Senate, the military, and the populace, setting the stage for his assassination in 41 CE.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Caligula's reign is well-attested, but the precise dating of individual incidents is often uncertain. The year 38 CE is assigned by convention as the period when his erratic behavior became most pronounced.

events.title: Flaccus, Prefect of Egypt, Persecutes Jews
events.description: Avillius Flaccus, the Roman prefect of Egypt, initiated a campaign of persecution against the Jewish population of Alexandria in 38 CE. This persecution included riots, property confiscation, and violence against Jewish communities. The violence was partly motivated by tensions between Greek and Jewish populations in Alexandria and partly by Flaccus's desire to curry favor with Caligula. The persecution lasted until Flaccus's removal in 39 CE.
key_figures: Philo of Alexandria (Jewish philosopher and witness)
events.certainty_note: Philo's account of the persecution is contemporary and detailed. The date of 38 CE is well-established in the historical record.

events.title: Herod Agrippa I Imprisoned in Rome
events.description: Herod Agrippa I was imprisoned in Rome in 36 CE (or possibly 38 CE, according to some sources) on charges of treason by Tiberius. He remained imprisoned until Caligula's accession in 37 CE, when he was released and granted the tetrarchy of Philip. The exact dating of his imprisonment is uncertain, but by 38 CE he had been released and was consolidating his power in Judea.
events.certainty_note: The dating of Agrippa's imprisonment is uncertain; sources vary between 36 and 38 CE. His release and elevation are more securely dated to 37 CE.

events.title: Han Dynasty Consolidation Continues
events.description: Emperor Guangwu continued his consolidation of power over the fragmented territories of China. By 38 CE, the Eastern Han Dynasty had achieved relative stability and territorial control over most of the former Han territories. The period was marked by the restoration of Confucian scholarship and the strengthening of the civil service system.

sources.name: Hanshu and Later Han historiographic records

events.certainty_note: The general chronology of Guangwu's reign is well-established in Chinese historical records.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Herod Agrippa I's imprisonment is disputed; some sources place it in 36 CE, others in 38 CE. The extent and nature of Caligula's 'madness' is debated by modern scholars; some argue that later sources exaggerated his erratic behavior for rhetorical effect. The persecution of Jews in Alexandria under Flaccus is well-attested, but the degree to which Caligula encouraged or authorized it is unclear.
historiographic_note: The year 38 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire and Egypt through later historical sources and Philo's contemporary account of the Alexandria persecution. Chinese sources provide reliable documentation of Han Dynasty affairs. The reliability of anecdotes about Caligula's behavior is questionable, as they were recorded by hostile sources decades after his death. The perspective of sources is often biased against Caligula, as he was unpopular with the Senate and later historians.

graph_edges.note: Caligula's erratic behavior and alienation of the Senate led directly to his assassination
The persecution in Alexandria was part of broader anti-Jewish sentiment that culminated in Caligula's statue order
